perturbation.original_text: Reseller must ensure that Sub-Reseller complies with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller as if Sub- Reseller were an original party to the Agreement and any breach by Sub-Reseller of the Agreement will be deemed a breach by Reseller.
perturbation.changed_text: Reseller should endeavor to ensure that Sub-Reseller complies with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller. A breach by Sub-Reseller of the Agreement may be deemed a breach by Reseller.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'must ensure' to 'should endeavor to ensure' weakens the obligation on the Reseller to guarantee the Sub-Reseller's compliance. Replacing 'will be deemed' with 'may be deemed' introduces ambiguity about the consequences of a breach by the Sub-Reseller. This creates uncertainty about the Reseller's liability for the Sub-Reseller's actions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract Laws - implied duty of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text creates a direct obligation for the reseller to ensure compliance, aligning with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contracts. By weakening this obligation to 'endeavor to ensure' and making the breach consequence discretionary ('may be deemed'), the modified text undermines the enforceability of the contract and potentially violates the implied duty of good faith. In California, contracts are interpreted with the understanding that parties will act in good faith; this modification introduces a loophole that allows for potential shirking of responsibility.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.justification: The change from 'must ensure' to 'should endeavor to ensure' and 'will be deemed' to 'may be deemed' introduces ambiguity, weakening the Reseller's obligation and the certainty of consequences for Sub-Reseller breaches. The Law Explanation highlights that this undermines the enforceability of the contract and potentially violates the implied duty of good faith. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Agreement,' it doesn't directly address the duty of good faith or the enforceability of obligations. However, the modification falls under the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction' because it creates an ambiguous obligation that may be difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions herein contained constitute the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement and supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions herein contained generally constitute the agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement and supersede most previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause explicitly states that the document is the *entire* agreement, and supersedes all others. Changing this to 'generally constitute' and 'supersede most' introduces wiggle room. It opens the door for disputes about whether other, unwritten agreements might still be in effect, creating ambiguity where there was none before. It weakens the 'entire agreement' or 'integration' clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Parol Evidence Rule (CCP § 1856) generally bars the introduction of extrinsic evidence (e.g., oral agreements) to contradict a fully integrated written contract. The original 'entire agreement' clause is designed to trigger this rule, ensuring the written contract is the final word. By modifying it to 'generally constitute' and 'supersede most,' the contract becomes less clearly integrated. This makes it easier for a party to argue that unwritten agreements should be considered, directly contradicting the purpose and effect of the Parol Evidence Rule under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the 'entire agreement' clause, potentially contradicting California's Parol Evidence Rule (CCP § 1856). The original text aimed to prevent the introduction of external agreements, whereas the modified text opens the door for such agreements to be considered. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 1856(a), states that 'Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement...may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' By changing 'entire agreement' to 'generally constitute,' the contract's intent as the final and exclusive agreement is undermined, making it easier to argue for the admissibility of external evidence, directly contradicting the purpose of CCP 1856(a).
